chapters.title: Passato Prossimo
chapters.description: Talk about completed past actions using Italy's most common past tense.

lessons.prompt: What is the passato prossimo of 'mangiare' for 'io'?
lessons.explanation: Passato prossimo = avere/essere + past participle. 'Mangiare' uses avere: ho mangiato.

lessons.prompt: Lei ___ andata al mercato. (She went to the market.)
lessons.explanation: Movement verbs use essere. 'Andare' → è andata. Note: past participle agrees with subject gender.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'We arrived late yesterday'
lessons.hint: 'Arrivare' uses essere. Noi → siamo. Past participle: arrivati (masculine plural).

lessons.prompt: What is the past participle of 'leggere' (to read)?
lessons.explanation: Irregular past participles: leggere→letto, scrivere→scritto, fare→fatto, dire→detto, vedere→visto.

lessons.prompt: Non ho ___ niente di interessante. (I didn't see anything interesting.)
lessons.explanation: 'Vedere' → visto. 'Non ho visto niente' = I haven't/didn't see anything. Double negative is standard in Italian.

chapters.title: Imperfetto
chapters.description: Use the imperfect tense to describe past habits and ongoing situations.

lessons.prompt: When do you use the imperfetto vs passato prossimo?
lessons.explanation: Imperfetto = ongoing/habitual past (used to, was doing). Passato prossimo = completed action.

lessons.prompt: Da bambino, ___ sempre la pizza. (As a child, I always ate pizza.)
lessons.explanation: Habitual past action → imperfetto. 'Mangiavo' = I used to eat / I was eating.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'While she was reading, the phone rang'
lessons.correctAnswer: Mentre lei leggeva il telefono squillò
lessons.hint: Background action (reading) = imperfetto. Sudden event (rang) = passato prossimo/remoto.

lessons.prompt: What is the imperfetto of 'essere' for 'io'?
lessons.explanation: Essere imperfetto (irregular): ero, eri, era, eravamo, eravate, erano.

lessons.prompt: Ogni estate, loro ___ al mare. (Every summer, they went to the sea.)
lessons.explanation: Recurring summer habit → imperfetto. 'Andare' imperfetto loro = andavano.

chapters.title: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns
chapters.description: Use lo, la, li, le, mi, ti, gli, le correctly in Italian sentences.

lessons.prompt: What does 'Lo vedo' mean?
lessons.explanation: 'Lo' = direct object pronoun for masculine singular. 'Lo vedo' = I see him/it.

lessons.prompt: ___ scrivo una email. (I am writing her an email.)
lessons.explanation: 'Le' = indirect object pronoun for her (feminine singular). 'Le scrivo' = I write to her.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'I call them every day' (masculine them)
lessons.hint: 'Li' = direct object pronoun for masculine plural. Goes before the verb.

lessons.prompt: Where do object pronouns go with infinitives?
lessons.explanation: With infinitives, drop final -e and attach: 'Voglio vederlo' = I want to see him.

lessons.prompt: Mi ___ che arriverà domani. (He tells me he will arrive tomorrow.)
lessons.explanation: 'Mi dice' = he tells me. 'Mi' = indirect object (to me). 'Dice' = he/she tells (dire).

chapters.title: Future Tense
chapters.description: Talk about future plans and make predictions in Italian.

lessons.prompt: What is the future tense of 'parlare' for 'io'?
L'anno prossimo, noi ___ in Italia. (Next year, we will go to Italy.)
lessons.explanation: 'Andare' has irregular future stem: andr-. Noi form: andremo.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'It will probably rain tomorrow'
lessons.hint: Italian uses the future tense to express probability. 'Pioverà' = it will rain.

lessons.prompt: What is the irregular future of 'essere'?
lessons.explanation: Essere future (irregular): sarò, sarai, sarà, saremo, sarete, saranno.

lessons.prompt: Quando ___ a Roma, visiterò il Colosseo. (When I arrive in Rome, I'll visit the Colosseum.)
lessons.explanation: In Italian, after 'quando' referring to future, use future tense (not present like English).
